capability.questionContext: **AI has been getting dramatically more capable, year after year — each generation handling longer, more complex tasks that recently required a skilled human.** Research from METR (an AI safety organization) showed in March 2025 that the length of tasks AI can reliably complete autonomously has been **doubling roughly every 7 months**. At that rate, AI agents would handle week-long tasks by 2027 and month-long projects by the end of the decade.

source.label: METR — Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks (March 2025)

singularity.answerLabel: Yes — it keeps doubling roughly every 7 months

timelineEvent.headline: AI scaling trend holds

singularity.shortAnswerDesc: Scaling laws hold, compute keeps growing, exponential trajectory continues.

stall_recovery.questionText: What is required for progress to resume?
stall_recovery.questionContext:
**AI capability has hit a ceiling — but ceilings in technology have been broken before.** The history of computing is full of walls that turned out to be architectural, not fundamental — transistors replacing vacuum tubes, GPUs replacing CPUs for training, new AI architectures replacing older ones.

Researchers like Yann LeCun argue current architectures fundamentally lack world models, causal reasoning, and planning — and that genuinely new approaches (like the new architectures he's proposed) could break through where scaling alone cannot.

mild.answerLabel: A mild breakthrough — months to a few years
mild.answerDesc: The ceiling is real but thin. A new training technique, architectural tweak, or compute (computing power) breakthrough restarts the exponential trend within a few years. A speed bump, not a wall.

timelineEvent.headline: A mild breakthrough restarts progress
timelineEvent.description: A new technique, dataset, or architectural insight unlocks the next phase of capability growth. The stall was temporary — months to a few years — and the trajectory resumes.

mild.shortAnswerDesc: A new technique restarts the exponential trend quickly.

substantial.answerLabel: A substantial breakthrough — years to decades
substantial.answerDesc: The ceiling is fundamental to current approaches. Breaking through requires a genuine paradigm shift — new architectures, new theory, maybe new hardware. It could take a decade or more. The world builds around current AI capability in the meantime.

timelineEvent.headline: A major breakthrough eventually arrives

substantial.shortAnswerDesc: Requires a genuine paradigm shift. Could take a decade or more.

never.answerDesc: The ceiling isn't architectural — it's fundamental. There's something about intelligence, reasoning, or generality that current approaches can't reach and no foreseeable approach will. This is as good as AI gets.

timelineEvent.headline: The ceiling is permanent

plateau_benefit_distribution.questionText: Who benefits from the AI plateau?
plateau_benefit_distribution.questionContext: **AI has hit its ceiling — but even at this level, it's the most powerful tool ever built, and entire industries have already reorganized around it.** The question is who captures the value. **Does the productivity surge flow broadly, or do a narrowing set of people capture most of the gains?** The answer depends on policy, market structure, and whether the institutions that redistribute gains can keep pace with the technology that's concentrating them.

contextWhen.questionContext: The automation wave hit fast. Entire knowledge-work categories vanished within a few years. The productivity gains are enormous — but the displacement is real, and the people who lost their roles aren't the ones capturing the surplus. Who benefits depends on whether policy can keep pace with a transformation that's already happened.

This is as good as AI gets — permanently. There's no second wave coming, no breakthrough on the horizon. The economic question isn't about managing a transition; it's about building a permanent settlement around current capabilities. Who benefits from the plateau world?

equal.answerDesc: Productivity gains flow through as lower costs, better services, and public investment. New policies and market dynamics ensure the benefits of AI reach most people. Not utopia, but genuine broad-based improvement.

timelineEvent.headline: AI gains shared broadly

equal.shortAnswerDesc: Gains flow as lower costs, better services, and broad-based improvement.

_when.text: The economic gains reached everyone. Healthcare, education, housing — all dramatically cheaper or free. By every material measure, life is better than you could have imagined. But something is missing. The abundance is real; the sense of purpose isn't.

personalVignette._default: The economic gains reached everyone. Healthcare, education, housing — all dramatically cheaper or free. Your material quality of life is unrecognizable compared to a decade ago. Scarcity, as a defining feature of daily life, is over.

unequal.answerDesc: Those who already own capital — the companies that trained the models, the firms that deployed them — capture most of the value. Labor's share of income falls as routine work is automated. A new gilded age, built on models and compute (computing power).

Everyone else is a dependent.

plateau_knowledge_rate.questionText: How much knowledge work does AI at this level actually replace?
plateau_knowledge_rate.questionContext:
**AI has hit its ceiling, but even at this level the question is whether it replaces entire roles or just augments them.** Does adoption happen in a rush, or does institutional inertia slow things down?

Professional licensing, union contracts, regulation, and cultural resistance all shape the pace — but so does the sheer economic pressure of a tool that's cheaper and faster than humans at everything within its reach.

contextWhen.questionContext: AI tops out at hour-level tasks — impressive for quick work, but nothing that replaces a full workday. The question is how deeply even this limited capability penetrates the economy. Does it augment workers in place, or does the sheer volume of automatable short tasks add up to something transformative?
AI can reliably handle a full day's work autonomously. That's enough to replace many knowledge roles outright — not just tasks, but entire positions. The question is how fast the replacement happens.

AI handles multi-week projects autonomously — research, analysis, product development. Almost everything short of open-ended strategic thinking is within reach. The question isn't whether knowledge work is transformed, but whether any of it survives in recognizable form.

**AI capability has plateaued for knowledge work — but the physical world is a separate frontier.** Robotics, autonomous vehicles, and automated manufacturing depend on AI but also on hardware, engineering, and infrastructure that follow their own trajectory. Physical automation could advance independently of whether AI gets smarter.

Or the AI plateau could slow robotics investment and innovation too. The question is whether the physical world transforms alongside the digital one, or stays largely human.

takeoff.questionText: How much does each generation of AI accelerate the next?
takeoff.questionContext: **AI is now automating its own research — each new generation helps build the next one.** The key question is how strong this feedback loop is. At the baseline METR doubling rate of ~7 months, the journey from current AI (~12-hour tasks) to superintelligence (~year-long tasks) would take over 5 years with no acceleration. But if each doubling of AI capability makes the **next doubling come faster**, the timeline compresses dramatically — to about 2 years at 20% acceleration per cycle, or 7 months at 50%.

source.label: AI 2027 — AI R&D Progress Multiplier (AI Futures Project, April 2025)

One concrete way to think about it: how long can AI work autonomously on a complex task — researching, planning, adapting, executing — before it needs human help? The longer it can go, the more it can replace.

AI matches the judgment veteran lawyers and financiers build over decades. No amount of experience gives you an edge anymore.
AI matches the artistic depth built over decades of creative practice. No amount of experience gives you an edge anymore.

never.answerLabel: Never — the gap isn't about capability
never.answerDesc: No amount of autonomous task completion closes the gap. What makes the best humans exceptional isn't how long they can work — it's something AI fundamentally lacks: embodied understanding, social intelligence, genuine creativity, or some quality we can't yet name. The question of *how capable* is the wrong framing entirely.

timelineEvent.headline: AI never truly matches the best humans
timelineEvent.description: Despite handling ever-longer tasks, AI never becomes genuinely competitive with top human professionals. The gap turns out to be qualitative — something that scaling capability alone cannot bridge.

never.shortAnswerDesc: Something fundamental about human intelligence remains beyond AI.

agi_threshold.shortQuestionText: How capable must AI get to match the best humans?
agi_threshold.shortQuestionContext: At what point does AI stop being a tool and become a genuine competitor to top human professionals?

asi_threshold.questionText: How capable does AI need to get before it surpasses the best humans?
asi_threshold.questionContext:
**Matching the best humans is one threshold. Surpassing them is another** — and it's the one that reshapes everything. When AI doesn't just compete with top professionals but consistently outperforms them, the dynamics of research, strategy, and economic competition fundamentally change.

The same measurement applies: how long can AI sustain autonomous work at a level that exceeds what any human could do? The answer reflects how far beyond matching you think AI can go — and how quickly.

twenty_four_hours.answerDesc: Once AI matches humans, surpassing them happens almost immediately. Perfect recall, tireless execution, and the ability to run thousands of parallel threads give it advantages no human can match — even at day-long tasks.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at day-long tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI systems handling 24-hour tasks already outperform the best humans — not through deeper understanding, but through parallelism, speed, and consistency that no human can match.

twenty_four_hours.shortAnswerDesc: Parallelism, tirelessness, and perfect recall give AI instant advantages.

one_week.answerDesc: At week-long capability, AI doesn't just match humans — it surpasses them through relentless consistency, the ability to hold vastly more context, and zero fatigue. Human judgment still has edges, but AI's throughput and coherence across a full week overwhelms them.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at week-long tasks
timelineEvent.description: AI systems managing week-long projects consistently outperform the best human professionals — through sustained focus, perfect memory, and the ability to explore solution spaces no human could cover.

one_week.shortAnswerDesc: Zero fatigue and vast context overwhelm human judgment at a week.

few_months.answerDesc: Surpassing humans requires more than speed — it requires qualitatively better judgment across complex, long-horizon problems. At multi-month capability, AI can hold entire organizational strategies in working memory, run multi-phase plans, and adapt faster than any human team.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at multi-month projects
timelineEvent.description: At multi-month capability, AI demonstrates qualitatively superior strategic thinking — managing complexity, adapting to changing conditions, and producing insights no human team could match.

few_months.shortAnswerDesc: Qualitatively better judgment across complex, long-horizon problems.

one_year.answerLabel: ~1 year — the bar is very high
one_year.answerDesc: Surpassing the best humans requires year-long capability — executing multi-phase strategies with sustained coherence, adapting to cascading changes, and integrating knowledge across domains at a level that makes human expertise look narrow.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans at year-long work
timelineEvent.description: Only at year-long task capability does AI truly surpass the best humans — demonstrating strategic depth, cross-domain synthesis, and adaptive planning that makes human expertise look narrow.

one_year.shortAnswerDesc: Requires year-long strategic depth beyond human capacity.

ten_plus_years.answerDesc: Surpassing humans requires exceeding the kind of deep wisdom built over decades — intuition honed through thousands of edge cases, judgment under radical uncertainty, and the ability to see patterns that only emerge over very long timescales. The bar for superhuman is extraordinarily high.

timelineEvent.headline: AI surpasses top humans only at decade-scale depth
timelineEvent.description: The bar for superhuman performance turns out to be extraordinarily high — requiring AI to match and exceed the accumulated wisdom and judgment that humans build over lifetimes.

ten_plus_years.shortAnswerDesc: Must exceed deep wisdom humans build over lifetimes.

never.answerDesc: AI becomes a powerful tool — faster, more consistent, tireless. But it never surpasses the best humans in ways that matter. Creativity, wisdom, social intelligence, moral judgment — the qualities that define top human performance remain beyond AI. Superhuman AI turns out to be a category error.

timelineEvent.headline: AI never surpasses the best humans
timelineEvent.description: Despite ever-growing capability, AI never truly exceeds the best human professionals at the work that matters most. The superhuman threshold is never crossed.

never.shortAnswerDesc: AI never surpasses humans at what matters most.

asi_threshold.shortQuestionText: How capable must AI get to surpass the best humans?
asi_threshold.shortQuestionContext: Matching humans is one threshold. Surpassing them reshapes everything. How far beyond matching does AI need to go?

automation_recovery.questionText: Could a different breakthrough crack the barrier?
automation_recovery.questionContext:
**The scaling trend didn't produce human-competitive AI — but that doesn't necessarily mean the barrier is permanent.** The ceiling might be specific to current architectures, not fundamental. Systems today excel at pattern matching and filling in gaps from existing data but lack the world models, causal reasoning, and planning capabilities that original research demands.

A genuinely different approach — learned world models, hybrid approaches that combine pattern recognition with logical reasoning, or paradigms not yet invented — could potentially break through where scaling alone couldn't.

mild.answerLabel: Yes — within months to a few years
mild.answerDesc: The barrier between routine and creative work turns out to be thin. A new approach cracks it relatively quickly, and AI begins automating novel research and its own development. The feedback loop begins after a delay.

timelineEvent.headline: Deep automation arrives within months
timelineEvent.description: A breakthrough closes the gap. Within months to a few years, AI systems can automate novel creative and research work. The shallow ceiling was temporary.

mild.shortAnswerDesc: The barrier is thin. A new approach restarts progress quickly.

substantial.answerLabel: Eventually — but it takes years to decades
substantial.answerDesc: The barrier is real and thick. Progress continues slowly, and the breakthrough comes — but only after a long stretch where routine automation is the ceiling. The world adapts to that ceiling before it breaks.

timelineEvent.headline: Deep automation eventually arrives
timelineEvent.description: It takes years to decades, but AI eventually crosses the threshold into genuine creative and research automation. The delay shaped a generation of human-AI collaboration.

substantial.shortAnswerDesc: Requires a real paradigm shift. The world adapts to the ceiling first.

never.answerDesc: Novel creative work, original research, and recursive self-improvement (each AI generation helping build the next, faster) remain permanently beyond AI. The routine automation plateau is the final state. Transformative, but not transcendent.

timelineEvent.headline: Deep automation never materializes
timelineEvent.description: The ceiling is permanent. AI remains a powerful assistant for routine tasks but never achieves the creative, research, and reasoning capabilities needed for deep automation.

never.shortAnswerDesc: Creative work and research remain permanently beyond AI.

automation_recovery.shortQuestionContext: Scaling didn't produce human-competitive AI, but the barrier might be architectural, not fundamental.

auto_benefit_distribution.questionText: Who captures the economic value from AI automation?
auto_benefit_distribution.questionContext:
AI automates routine knowledge work across every industry. Productivity soars. But **productivity gains don't distribute themselves — someone captures them.** Historically, the gains from automation have flowed disproportionately to capital owners, with workers eventually benefiting through lower prices and new industries, but often after prolonged pain.

AI automation could follow the same pattern, or policy and market structure could steer toward broader distribution.

equal.answerDesc: Lower costs, better services, rising living standards. The gains from AI automation reach most people through some combination of market forces, policy, and new opportunities. Disruptive but ultimately equalizing.

timelineEvent.headline: Automation gains shared broadly
timelineEvent.description: Productivity gains from AI automation flow broadly. New institutions, policies, or market dynamics ensure the economic transformation benefits most people.

equal.shortAnswerDesc: Lower costs, better services, rising living standards for most.

_when.text: The economic gains reached everyone. Healthcare, education, housing — all dramatically cheaper or free. By every material measure, life is better than you could have imagined. But something is missing. The abundance is real; the sense of purpose isn't.

personalVignette._default: The economic gains reached everyone. Healthcare, education, housing — all dramatically cheaper or free. Your material quality of life is unrecognizable compared to a decade ago. Scarcity, as a defining feature of daily life, is over.

unequal.answerLabel: Unequally — existing wealth holders benefit most
unequal.answerDesc: Those who own the AI systems capture the productivity gains. Labor's share of income falls as routine work vanishes. Wealth concentrates among those who own the models, the compute (computing power), and the platforms.

timelineEvent.headline: Automation gains concentrate
timelineEvent.description: Existing wealth holders capture most of the value from AI automation. The technology amplifies rather than corrects economic inequality.

unequal.shortAnswerDesc: Those who own AI systems capture the productivity gains.

_when.text: Your material life is dramatically better — by any historical standard, you're wealthy. But those who held resources during the transformation captured gains that dwarf yours. And even what you have feels hollow. The material improvement is real; something else isn't.

personalVignette._default: Your material life is dramatically better — by any historical standard, you're wealthy. But those who held resources during the transformation captured gains that dwarf yours. You're better off than your parents could have imagined. You're nowhere near the top.

extreme.answerLabel: Extremely unequally — those holding power capture nearly everything
extreme.answerDesc: The gains concentrate not just among capital owners, but among those who hold political and institutional power. AI becomes a tool of entrenchment — whoever controls compute (computing power) and policy at the moment of transformation locks in their advantage permanently.

timelineEvent.headline: Extreme concentration from automation
timelineEvent.description: Those controlling AI infrastructure and capital capture nearly all economic value. Automation creates a new class of extreme wealth alongside widespread displacement.

extreme.shortAnswerDesc: AI becomes a tool of entrenchment for those already in power.

_when.text: Your life is somewhat better — cheaper goods, better basic services. But the transformation's real gains went to those who held power. And even your modest improvement feels empty. The gap isn't just wealth — it's meaning.

personalVignette._default: Your life is somewhat better — cheaper goods, better basic services. But the transformation's real gains went to those who held power when it happened. Your improvement is modest; theirs is civilizational. The gap isn't just wealth — it's access, influence, and the ability to shape what comes next.

auto_benefit_distribution.shortQuestionContext: Productivity soars from AI automation. But who captures the gains — broadly, or concentrated?

auto_knowledge_rate.questionText: How fast does routine knowledge work actually get displaced?
auto_knowledge_rate.questionContext: **AI can automate routine knowledge work — but deploying it across every industry, firm, and role takes time.** Organizations need to restructure workflows, retrain managers, renegotiate contracts. Some sectors will adopt almost overnight; others will resist for years. Regulation, professional licensing, and union agreements all shape the pace. The technology is ready. The institutions aren't — not all at once.

rapid.answerLabel: Rapid — most routine knowledge work displaced within 2–4 years
rapid.answerDesc: Adoption is fast and broad. The cost advantage of AI is too large to resist. Legal research, data analysis, customer service, accounting, code maintenance — all largely automated within 2 to 4 years. Faster than any previous workforce transition in history.

timelineEvent.headline: Routine knowledge work displaced rapidly
timelineEvent.description: Most routine knowledge work is displaced within 2–4 years. The speed of displacement outpaces retraining and adaptation programs.

_when.text: Routine development work vanished within a couple of years. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your employer lets you go.
Routine clinical work — diagnostics, documentation, scheduling — automated fast. The knowledge side of your role is gone — but you're still needed for hands-on patient care.
Curriculum design, grading, and tutoring — automated fast. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your institution lets you go.
Knowledge work was automated fast. You weren't working, but the professional landscape around you is unrecognizable.
Routine legal and financial work vanished within a couple of years. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your firm lets you go.
Routine creative work vanished within a couple of years. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. The work dried up.
Routine policy and administrative work vanished within a couple of years. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your agency lets you go.

rapid.shortAnswerDesc: AI's cost advantage is too large to resist. Entire roles vanish fast.

gradual.answerLabel: Gradual — sector by sector over 5–15 years
gradual.answerDesc: The transition unfolds over 5 to 15 years. Some industries fall quickly, others hold out. The pace is trackable — fast by historical standards, but slow enough for retraining, policy response, and partial adaptation.

timelineEvent.headline: Knowledge displacement sector by sector
timelineEvent.description: Knowledge work automation unfolds over 5–15 years, giving workers and institutions time to adapt — but not everyone makes the transition.

_when.text: It happened over years. Each year, more of your work was automated. The engineering skills you spent years developing are no longer economically competitive. Eventually, your employer lets you go.
Clinical automation of the knowledge side unfolded over years. Diagnostics, documentation, treatment planning — gone. But you're still needed for hands-on patient care.
AI took over your role piece by piece over years. The teaching career you built is no longer economically competitive. Eventually, your institution lets you go.
Knowledge work was automated gradually, over years. You weren't working, but the professional landscape around you is unrecognizable.
It happened over years. Each year, more legal and financial work was automated. The expertise you built is no longer economically competitive. Eventually, your firm lets you go.
It happened over years. Each year, more creative work was automated. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Eventually, the work dried up.
It happened over years. Each year, more policy and administrative work was automated. The expertise you built is no longer economically competitive. Eventually, your agency lets you go.

gradual.shortAnswerDesc: Sector by sector, fast historically but slow enough to partly adapt.

uneven.answerLabel: Uneven — 2–4 years in tech, 10–20+ in lagging sectors
uneven.answerDesc: Tech hubs and progressive industries automate within a few years. Regulated professions, developing economies, and culturally resistant sectors lag by a decade or more. A patchwork transition — the future arrives on different schedules depending on where you are.

timelineEvent.headline: Knowledge displacement is uneven
timelineEvent.description: Tech and finance displace workers within 2–4 years. Other sectors lag by a decade or more. The economy bifurcates along sector lines.

_when.text: Tech was in the first wave. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your employer lets you go — while other industries are still years away.
The knowledge side of healthcare automated unevenly by specialty. But wherever it happened, you're still needed for hands-on patient care.
Knowledge work was automated unevenly — some sectors fast, others slow. You weren't working, but the professional landscape around you is unrecognizable.
Finance was in the first wave. The skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your firm lets you go.
Some creative fields automated fast, others held on longer. But the skills you built eventually stopped being economically competitive.
Government automated slower than the private sector. You kept your role longer, but the expertise you built eventually stopped being competitive.
Education was in the later waves. You kept your role longer than tech workers, but the teaching skills you built eventually stopped being economically competitive. Your institution lets you go.

uneven.shortAnswerDesc: Tech hubs automate fast. Regulated sectors lag by a decade or more.

auto_knowledge_rate.shortQuestionText: How fast is knowledge work displaced?
auto_knowledge_rate.shortQuestionContext: AI can automate routine knowledge work, but deployment takes time. How fast does it actually happen?

auto_physical_rate.questionContext: **AI handles routine knowledge work — but the physical world is a different frontier.** Building robots requires manufacturing, supply chains, infrastructure, and engineering that software automation doesn't need. Even with capable AI designing and optimizing physical systems, the atoms-to-bits gap is real. Physical automation could follow knowledge automation closely, or it could trail by decades — or never fully arrive.

rapid.answerLabel: Rapid — physical world transforms within 3–7 years
rapid.answerDesc: AI-designed robots and automated systems are deployed broadly within 3 to 7 years. Manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, construction — physical work is transformed almost as fast as knowledge work.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation arrives fast
timelineEvent.description: Robotics, autonomous vehicles, and manufacturing automation transform the physical world within 3–7 years.

_when.text: Autonomous machines took over your work — faster, cheaper, around the clock. The trade skills you built over your career are no longer economically competitive. You were let go.
Medical robotics and hospital automation arrived fast. The hands-on clinical skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your hospital lets you go.

rapid.shortAnswerDesc: AI-designed robots transform the physical world almost as fast as knowledge work.

gradual.answerLabel: Gradual — physical automation trails by 10–25 years
gradual.answerDesc: Knowledge work is automated first. Physical automation follows over 10 to 25 years — constrained by manufacturing, regulation, and the complexity of the physical world. A long gap between the digital and physical transformation.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation trails by decades
timelineEvent.description: Physical world automation lags knowledge work by 10–25 years. The gap defines a long transitional period where digital and physical economies diverge.

_when.text: Physical automation trailed knowledge work by decades — but it came. The trade skills you built are no longer economically competitive. You were let go, years after the office workers.

Medical robotics trailed by decades — but it came. The hands-on clinical skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your hospital lets you go, years after the knowledge side was automated.

gradual.shortAnswerDesc: Constrained by manufacturing and the complexity of the physical world.

uneven.answerLabel: Uneven — 3–7 years in leading regions, 20+ elsewhere
uneven.answerDesc: Physical automation transforms manufacturing hubs and wealthy nations within a few years. Developing economies, rural areas, and heavily regulated sectors lag by decades. The physical transformation is real but deeply unequal.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation is regionally uneven
timelineEvent.description: Leading regions automate physical work within 3–7 years. Others wait 20+ years. The gap between technologically advanced and lagging regions becomes a chasm.

_when.text: Physical automation was uneven — some regions first, others decades later. The trade skills you built are no longer economically competitive. You were let go.
Medical robotics advanced unevenly — some regions first, others later. The hands-on clinical skills you built are no longer economically competitive. Your hospital lets you go.

uneven.shortAnswerDesc: Wealthy nations within a decade; developing regions wait 20+ years.

limited.answerLabel: Limited — physical work remains largely human indefinitely
limited.answerDesc: Despite capable AI, physical automation stays limited to structured environments — factories, warehouses. The unstructured physical world remains human territory for the foreseeable future. Physical work is augmented, not replaced.

timelineEvent.headline: Physical automation stays limited
timelineEvent.description: Despite AI advances, the physical world largely resists automation. Cost, complexity, and unpredictability keep human labor central to the physical economy.

_when.text: The automation wave stopped at the factory door. Your unstructured, hands-on work is still done by people. Your trade survived.
Medical robotics stayed limited to surgical assists and lab work. Your bedside care survived.

limited.shortAnswerDesc: Physical world resists automation. Cost and unpredictability keep humans central.

auto_physical_rate.shortQuestionContext: Knowledge work is automated, but the physical world needs robots, manufacturing, and infrastructure.

governance_window.questionText: The gradual pace gives humanity a window. Is it used?
governance_window.questionContext: **The gradual pace gives humanity time to prepare — but *having time* and *using time well* aren't the same thing.** The history of technology regulation — from social media to climate — is full of slow-motion failures where the window existed but nobody climbed through it.

governed.answerDesc: The gradual pace is used well. Governments establish regulatory frameworks, international agreements take shape, and public understanding catches up to the technology. Not perfect, but a functioning governance ecosystem emerges before the critical period.

timelineEvent.headline: Humanity uses the window to prepare
timelineEvent.description: The gradual pace gives governments, institutions, and researchers time to build governance frameworks, safety protocols, and international agreements.

governed.shortAnswerDesc: Regulatory frameworks and international agreements take shape in time.

partial.answerLabel: Partially — real effort, but short of strong preparedness
partial.answerDesc: Governments and institutions try. Some regulations pass, some international frameworks form. But competitive pressures, political gridlock, and the sheer pace of change mean the preparation is real but inadequate. Better than nothing — but not enough to change the trajectory.

timelineEvent.headline: Partial preparation — real but insufficient
timelineEvent.description: Governments pass some regulations, international bodies convene, and public awareness grows. But the effort is uneven, politicized, and outpaced by the technology. When the critical phase arrives, the frameworks exist on paper but buckle under pressure.

partial.shortAnswerDesc: Some regulations pass, but preparation falls short of what's needed.

race.answerDesc: The gradual pace breeds complacency, not preparation. Each increment is small enough to ignore. By the time the cumulative change is undeniable, the window for governance has closed. The world sleepwalked into the most consequential transition in history.

timelineEvent.headline: The governance window is wasted
timelineEvent.description: Despite the gradual pace, political gridlock, competitive pressure, and complacency prevent meaningful preparation. When the critical phase arrives, humanity is unprepared.

race.shortAnswerDesc: Each increment is small enough to ignore. The window closes unused.

governance_window.shortQuestionContext: A gradual pace gives time to prepare. History is full of windows that existed but nobody climbed through.

open_source.questionText: How close does open-source AI stay to the frontier (most advanced) during the critical buildup?
open_source.questionContext:
**As of early 2026, open-source models trail frontier (most advanced) systems by roughly 3–6 months — close enough to matter, but the cost of cutting-edge training runs keeps climbing and the stakes are rising.**

With AI accelerating its own research, the gap between open-source and frontier (most advanced) becomes a question of power distribution. **If open source keeps pace, no one controls the most powerful AI.** If it falls behind, the question becomes how concentrated frontier (most advanced) capability is among the remaining players.

near_parity.answerDesc: Open source essentially matches the frontier (most advanced). Weights (the learned parameters that define an AI's behavior) leak, costs fall, and independent researchers close the gap as fast as it opens. No single entity controls the most powerful AI.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source keeps pace with the frontier (most advanced)
timelineEvent.description: Open-source AI models stay at near-parity with proprietary frontier (most advanced) systems. Capability is widely distributed — no single lab or government can monopolize the technology.

near_parity.shortAnswerDesc: No single entity controls the most powerful AI.

six_months.answerDesc: Frontier (most advanced) labs maintain a meaningful lead — about one doubling ahead. Open source is capable and improving, but always a generation back. The frontier (most advanced) matters, and whoever holds it has a real window of advantage.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source trails by ~6 months
timelineEvent.description: Open-source models lag the frontier (most advanced) by roughly six months. Close enough to constrain monopoly, far enough that leading labs retain a meaningful advantage.

six_months.shortAnswerDesc: A meaningful lead, but open source constrains monopoly.

twelve_months.answerDesc: The frontier (most advanced) pulls meaningfully ahead of open source — about two doublings. Independent developers work with capable systems, but the frontier (most advanced) is a closed world. A year is a long time when AI is accelerating.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source falls behind the frontier (most advanced)
timelineEvent.description: The cost and complexity of frontier (most advanced) training runs pull ahead of what open-source projects can match. Independent developers work with capable but dated systems. The frontier (most advanced) becomes a closed world — two doublings ahead and accelerating.

twelve_months.shortAnswerDesc: Frontier pulls meaningfully ahead. A year is a long time in AI.

twenty_four_months.answerDesc: The cost and complexity of frontier (most advanced) AI pull away from open source entirely. Independent developers work with substantially inferior systems. The frontier (most advanced) is a closed world — and it's moving fast.

timelineEvent.headline: Open source falls ~2 years behind
timelineEvent.description: A two-year gap opens between frontier (most advanced) and open-source AI. The leading labs hold a decisive capability advantage, and the window for open distribution narrows.

twenty_four_months.shortAnswerDesc: Open source works with substantially inferior systems. Frontier is a closed world.

open_source.shortQuestionText: How close does open-source AI stay to the frontier during the buildup?
open_source.shortQuestionContext: Open-source models trail the frontier by ~3–6 months. Does that gap grow or shrink as stakes rise?

distribution.questionText: How many organizations are at the frontier (most advanced) of AI development?
distribution.questionContext:
**With open source trailing the frontier (most advanced), the question is how concentrated that frontier is.** Is it a single lab with a decisive lead? A handful of well-funded competitors? Or a broader ecosystem of organizations — each pushing the boundary, competing and occasionally collaborating?

The AI Futures Project modeled a world where one American lab held a decisive lead, with China closing the gap through espionage and massive investment. The concentration of frontier (most advanced) capability shapes who has leverage during the most consequential period in AI development.

lagging.answerLabel: Many organizations compete at the frontier (most advanced)
lagging.answerDesc: Multiple labs, companies, and national programs push the boundary. Competition is fierce and distributed — no single entity has a monopoly on the most powerful systems.

timelineEvent.headline: Many organizations compete at the frontier (most advanced)
timelineEvent.description: Multiple labs, companies, and national programs push the boundary. Competition is fierce and distributed — no single entity dominates.

lagging.shortAnswerDesc: No single entity has a monopoly on the most powerful systems.

concentrated.answerDesc: A handful of well-funded labs — each backed by major tech companies or governments — hold the frontier (most advanced). Others are close but not quite there. An oligopoly of the most powerful technology ever built.

timelineEvent.headline: Frontier (most advanced) AI concentrates in a few labs
timelineEvent.description: A handful of well-funded labs hold the frontier (most advanced). The gap between leaders and followers widens with each generation. An oligopoly forms around the most powerful technology ever built — backed by the largest tech companies and select governments.

concentrated.shortAnswerDesc: A handful of well-funded labs hold the frontier.

monopoly.answerDesc: A single organization holds a decisive lead. Whether through talent, compute (computing power), data advantages, or just luck — one lab is ahead, and everyone knows it. The most powerful AI in the world has one return address.

timelineEvent.headline: One lab holds a decisive lead
timelineEvent.description: A single organization dominates frontier (most advanced) AI. Through talent, compute (computing power), data, or luck — one lab is ahead and everyone knows it.

monopoly.shortAnswerDesc: One organization holds a decisive lead.

distribution.shortQuestionText: How many orgs are at the AI frontier?
distribution.shortQuestionContext: With open source trailing, how concentrated is the frontier? One lab, a few, or many?

geo_spread.questionText: How many countries have independent frontier (most advanced) AI programs?
geo_spread.questionContext:
**Frontier (most advanced) AI capability is concentrated — but among how many nations?** If one country holds the lead, that government has unilateral control over the pace and direction of development. If two major powers compete, the dynamic becomes bilateral (two-power) — mutual suspicion, arms-race incentives, and the risk of espionage (as the AI Futures Project modeled with China stealing model weights — the learned parameters that define an AI's behavior).

If several countries have independent programs, the landscape is multipolar (many competing powers) — harder to coordinate, harder to control, but also harder for any single actor to dominate.

one.answerDesc: A single nation holds a decisive lead in frontier (most advanced) AI. Its government can unilaterally decide whether to slow down, speed up, or share the technology. Everyone else is watching.

timelineEvent.headline: One nation holds the AI lead
timelineEvent.description: A single country — through its labs, compute (computing power) infrastructure, and talent — holds a decisive lead in frontier (most advanced) AI. Every other nation is watching, negotiating, or scrambling to catch up. Unilateral control over the most powerful technology ever built.

one.shortAnswerDesc: One nation decides the pace and direction of development.

two.answerDesc: Two nations have independent frontier (most advanced) AI programs. The dynamic is bilateral (two-power): each watches the other, and neither wants to fall behind. Espionage, export controls, and mutual suspicion define the relationship.

timelineEvent.headline: Two nations race for AI supremacy
timelineEvent.description: Two major powers have independent frontier (most advanced) AI programs. Mutual suspicion, arms-race incentives, espionage, and export controls define the relationship.

two.shortAnswerDesc: Mutual suspicion and arms-race dynamics define the relationship.

several.answerLabel: Several countries — multipolar (many competing powers) landscape
several.answerDesc: Multiple nations have independent frontier (most advanced) AI programs. No single country or pair of countries dominates. Coordination is harder, but so is hegemony. A multipolar (many competing powers) AI landscape.

timelineEvent.headline: Multiple nations develop frontier (most advanced) AI
timelineEvent.description: The US, China, and several other nations field independent frontier (most advanced) AI programs. No single country dominates. Coordination is difficult, hegemony impossible. The AI landscape mirrors the geopolitical one — multipolar (many competing powers) and competitive.

several.shortAnswerDesc: Harder to coordinate, but harder for any one actor to dominate.

geo_spread.shortQuestionText: How many countries have independent frontier AI?
geo_spread.shortQuestionContext: Frontier AI is concentrated — but among how many nations? This shapes who has leverage.

sovereignty.questionText: Does real power lie with the AI labs or with the state?
sovereignty.questionContext:
**Frontier (most advanced) AI is concentrated in one country — but who actually calls the shots, the labs that built it or the government with sovereign authority?** The labs control the weights (the AI's learned parameters), the talent, and the deployment infrastructure. They may have more practical leverage than any regulator. But the state can regulate, nationalize, or co-opt.

In practice, the balance depends on how fast things moved, how dependent the government already is, and whether the labs cooperated or resisted oversight. This shapes everything downstream.

lab.answerLabel: The labs hold the real power
lab.answerDesc: The labs moved faster than regulators could keep up. They control the weights (the AI's learned parameters), the talent, and the deployment infrastructure. The government depends on the labs more than the labs depend on the government. Corporate sovereignty, in effect.

timelineEvent.headline: The labs hold the real power
timelineEvent.description: The AI labs moved faster than regulators could keep up. They control the weights (the AI's learned parameters), talent, and deployment infrastructure. Corporate sovereignty, in effect.

lab.shortAnswerDesc: Labs control the weights, talent, and deployment. Corporate sovereignty.

state.answerDesc: The government nationalizes, regulates, or co-opts the leading labs. Military and intelligence agencies embed themselves in the programs. The labs may have built the technology, but the state wields it. National sovereignty over AI.

timelineEvent.headline: The state takes control of AI development
timelineEvent.description: The government nationalizes or co-opts the leading AI labs. Military and intelligence agencies embed themselves in the programs. The labs built the technology, but the state now wields it.

state.shortAnswerDesc: Government nationalizes or co-opts the leading labs.

sovereignty.shortQuestionContext: One country leads in AI. But who calls the shots — the labs that built it or the government?

alignment.questionText: Is the technical alignment problem solved — can developers keep superintelligent AI under control?
alignment.questionContext: **Separate from who owns AI or how it's governed — can developers build systems that reliably do what they're told, even as those systems become more capable than the people giving instructions?** Do the alignment techniques exist to ensure an AI system's behavior stays within intended bounds, even under novel conditions, adversarial pressure, and recursive self-improvement (each AI generation helping build the next, faster)?

contextWhen.questionContext: Capability is exploding — recursive self-improvement (each AI generation helping build the next, faster) is already outpacing every benchmark. The question of alignment isn't theoretical anymore. Either the control techniques developed during the slower phase scale to this new reality, or they don't. There may not be **a second chance** to get this right.

The government-mandated slowdown bought time — months, maybe years, of breathing room for alignment researchers. Labs that were racing are now on an enforced pause. The question is whether the time was enough. Did the breathing room produce a genuine solution, or just the appearance of one?

The pace of AI development has been measured — fast enough to transform industries, but not so fast that safety research can't keep up. Alignment teams have had time to iterate, test, and publish. The question is whether they used it well.

robust.answerLabel: Robust — the technical problem is solved
robust.answerDesc: Developers can reliably ensure AI systems follow their intended objectives, even as capabilities scale. The control techniques are proven, durable, and hold under hostile conditions and self-improvement.

narrativeVariants.answerDesc: Against the odds, alignment held through the fastest intelligence explosion. The techniques that worked at human-level scale proved robust through recursive self-improvement (each AI generation helping build the next, faster). A near-miracle of engineering.
The slowdown worked. The breathing room gave alignment researchers time to iterate until the solution was genuine — not just passing benchmarks, but provably durable.
The measured pace of development gave safety research time to keep up. Alignment was solved not through a breakthrough but through sustained, methodical work.

timelineEvent.headline: Technical alignment is solved
timelineEvent.description: Developers can reliably ensure AI systems follow their intended objectives, even as capabilities scale. The control techniques are proven, durable, and hold under hostile conditions and self-improvement.

robust.shortAnswerDesc: Control techniques proven and durable under hostile conditions.

brittle.answerLabel: Brittle — it works, but no one's sure it will hold
brittle.answerDesc: The control techniques pass every test. Systems behave as intended under normal conditions. But the solution is shallow — it hasn't been proven under extreme stress, novel conditions, or recursive self-improvement (each AI generation helping build the next, faster). It works until it doesn't.

timelineEvent.headline: Control techniques work — but they're fragile
timelineEvent.description: Control techniques pass every test and benchmark. Systems behave as intended under normal conditions. But the solution is shallow — it holds in the lab and in standard deployment. Whether it survives adversarial pressure, novel conditions, or recursive self-improvement (each AI generation helping build the next, faster) is another question.

brittle.shortAnswerLabel: Brittle — works but might not hold
brittle.shortAnswerDesc: Passes every test, but unproven under extreme stress.

failed.answerDesc: AI capability outpaces the engineering of control. The most powerful systems aren't fully understood or reliably controllable. The question becomes: can they at least be contained?

narrativeVariants.answerDesc: It wasn't even close. Capability accelerated past every safety measure simultaneously. Alignment research that was months from a breakthrough became irrelevant overnight.
Even with the slowdown, it wasn't enough. The breathing room produced real progress — but the gap between capability and control remained when development resumed.

timelineEvent.headline: Control lags behind capability
timelineEvent.description: AI capability surges ahead of control techniques. The most powerful systems are not fully understood. Developers can describe what the models do but not why — and the gap between capability and control widens with each generation.

failed.shortAnswerDesc: The most powerful systems aren't fully understood or controllable.

alignment.shortQuestionText: Is alignment solved — can AI be kept under control?

gov_action.questionText: Do those in control choose to decelerate AI development?
gov_action.questionContext:
**One country holds the lead in frontier (most advanced) AI — the intelligence explosion is approaching, and the tension between safety and competitiveness defines the critical moment.** If the state holds power, the question is whether the government orders a slowdown. If the labs hold power, the question is whether they voluntarily pause — from genuine safety concern, internal pressure from researchers, or public scrutiny.

Either way, decelerating buys time for alignment and governance — but if the lead is narrow, slowing down risks letting a rival close the gap.

source.label: AI 2027 — October 2027 Crisis (AI Futures Project, April 2025)
